title: Understanding Unit Fractions: One Part of a Whole

steps.subgoalLabel: Identify the whole and its parts
steps.instruction: A pie is cut into 6 equal slices. You take 1 slice. What fraction of the pie is that?
steps.work: The whole pie = 6 equal slices. You took 1 slice.
steps.explanation: To write a fraction, we need to know the total number of equal parts (denominator) and how many parts we're talking about (numerator).

steps.instruction: The denominator is the total number of equal parts the whole is divided into.
steps.explanation: The bottom number of a fraction tells us how many equal parts make up the whole.

steps.instruction: A unit fraction always has 1 as the numerator because we are talking about one part.
steps.explanation: The top number tells how many parts we have. In a unit fraction, it is always 1.
One slice out of six equal slices is 1/6 of the pie. This is a unit fraction because the numerator is 1.

title: Comparing Unit Fractions: Which Piece Is Bigger?

steps.work: We need to compare two unit fractions: 1/4 and 1/6.
steps.explanation: Both fractions have 1 in the numerator, so we only need to think about what the denominators tell us.

steps.subgoalLabel: Think about what the denominator means
steps.instruction: A denominator of 4 means the whole is cut into 4 equal parts. A denominator of 6 means 6 equal parts.
steps.work: 1/4 → whole split into 4 parts. 1/6 → whole split into 6 parts.
steps.explanation: The more parts you cut a whole into, the smaller each part becomes.

steps.instruction: Which gives bigger pieces: cutting into 4 parts or cutting into 6 parts?
steps.work: 4 parts → bigger pieces. 6 parts → smaller pieces. So 1/4 > 1/6.
steps.explanation: Fewer parts means each part is larger. Since 4 < 6, each fourth is larger than each sixth.
For unit fractions, the one with the smaller denominator is always the larger fraction.
